coredata.dc:title: Metal-pyrimidine nanocubes immobilized enzymes with pH-switchable multienzyme-like activity for broad-pH-responsive sensing assay for organophosphorus pesticides
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Changbao
Chen, Bo
Xing, Yifei
Zhao, Huimin
coredata.dc:description: Peroxidase (POD)-like can only function in acidic environments and the pH mismatch restricts the application of enzyme-nanozyme cascade catalytic sensing platforms in the broad-pH-responsive assay for organophosphorus pesticides (OPs). Herein, the metal-pyrimidine nanocubes (MPNCs) with intrinsic pH-switchable POD-like and catalase (CAT)-like properties were synthesized via the coordination of pyrimidin-2-ol with Cu2+. Meanwhile, acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and choline oxidase (CHO) were simultaneously encapsulated in MPNCs to construct an enzyme-nanozyme cascade catalytic platform (AChE/CHO@MPNCs). AChE/CHO@MPNCs could catalyze the hydrolysis of acetylcholine to choline, which was subsequently converted to H2O2. The POD-like activity of MPNCs was dominant under acidic conditions, while the CAT-like activity prevailed under neutral and alkaline conditions, which could catalyze H2O2 to •OH and O2, respectively, then oxidizing dopamine (DA) to polydopamine quantum dots (PDA QDs) with different fluorescence characteristics. Consequently, OPs could be detected in a linear range from 0.05 to 1000 nM with a LOD of 0.015 nM in acidic environments and a linear range from 0.05 to 500 nM with a LOD of 0.023 nM in alkaline environments. Overall, our work expands the horizon of constructing enzyme@MOFs composites with high catalytic activity. Meanwhile, the intrinsic pH-switchable multienzyme-like property opens avenues to construct sensing platforms with broad-pH-responsive for OPs and other analytes detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-pyrimidine nanocubes
Enzyme immobilization
PH-switchable multienzyme-like activity
Organophosphorus pesticides
Broad-pH-responsive

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of MPNCs and AChE/CHO@MPNCs
Determination of enzymic loading capacity, immobilization efficiency, and catalytic activity
pH-switchable POD-like and CAT-like properties of MPNCs
Feasibility of AChE/CHO@MPNCs with pH-switchable multienzyme-like characteristic for broad-pH-responsive sensing
Performance in fluorescent biosensing for OPs detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21976022) and the Dalian POCT laboratory.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of AChE/CHO@MPNCs: (A) SEM image; (B) TEM image; (C) EDS mapping image; (D) XRD patterns; (E) FTIR spectra; (F) XPS survey spectra, High-resolution XPS spectra of (G) C 1 s, (H) N 1 s, (I) O1s, and (J) Cu 2p in MPNCs; (K) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms; (L) Pore width distribution curves.

(A) Conversion of enzymatic activity after exposure to pyrimidin-2-ol solution and the equal amount of free enzyme as the control; (B) The water droplet contact angle of MPNCs; (C) FL spectra of free enzymes and the enzymes immobilized by MPNCs; CD bands of the free enzyme, after exposure to pyrimidin-2-ol solution, and after the encapsulation of MPNCs (D) AChE, (E) CHO; (F) Conversion of the catalytic activity of free enzymes and enzymes@MPNCs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of MPNCs/TMB/H2O2, MPNCs/TMB, TMB/H2O2, MPNCs /H2O2, MPNCs, H2O2, and TMB; (B) Digital photograph of the colorimetric reaction between TMB/H2O2 and MPNCs at different pH and (C) the corresponding UV-Vis spectra; (D) EPR spectra of •OH trapped by DMPO at pH 5.0; (E) Digital photographs of H2O2 reacting with MPNCs and (F) the corresponding dissolved oxygen in different pH environments.

(A) Feasibility of AChE/CHO@MPNCs in fluorescent biosensing for OPs detection under acidic and alkaline conditions; Characterization of PDA QDs polymerized under acidic conditions: (B) TEM images and size distribution (inset); (C) The HR-TEM images; (D) The corresponding UV–vis, FL excitation (Ex), and FL emission (Em) spectra (inset: photographs of PDA QDs taken under (left) visible light and (right) 365 nm UV light); (E) FL excitation-emission matrix for PDA QDs; Characterization of PDA QDs polymerized under alkaline conditions: (F) TEM images and size distribution (inset); (G) The HR-TEM images; (H) The corresponding UV–vis, FL excitation (Ex), and FL emission (Em) spectra (inset: photographs of PDA QDs taken under (left) visible light and (right) 365 nm UV light); (I) FL excitation-emission matrix for PDA QDs.

(A) FL spectra of the biosensing system in the presence of different OPs concentrations; (B) Linear relationship between the FL intensity and the logarithm of OPs concentration; (C) FL spectra of PDA QDs at different OPs concentrations; (D) Calibration plots toward different concentrations of OPs; (E) Selectivity of AChE/CHO@MPNCs for different substances; (F) Anti-interference performance of AChE/CHO@MPNCs for biological species and chemical electrolytes; (G) Universality for the detection of various OPs; (H) Stability of free enzymes and AChE/CHO/HRP@MPNFs after exposure to high temperature, chaotropic agent, proteolytic agent, chelating agent, organic solvent, acidic environment, alkaline environment, and storage stability; (I) Recyclability of AChE/CHO/HRP@MPNFs and free enzymes.
Schematic illustration of the construction of enzyme-nanozyme cascade catalytic platform with pH-switchable multienzyme-like activity and the mechanism of broad-pH-responsive sensing assay for OPs.

head.ce:title: Metal-pyrimidine nanocubes immobilized enzymes with pH-switchable multienzyme-like activity for broad-pH-responsive sensing assay for organophosphorus pesticides

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering (Ministry of Education, China), School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering (Ministry of Education, China), School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering (Ministry of Education, China), School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: No. 2 Linggong Road, Ganjingzi, Dalian, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Peroxidase (POD)-like can only function in acidic environments and the pH mismatch restricts the application of enzyme-nanozyme cascade catalytic sensing platforms in the broad-pH-responsive assay for organophosphorus pesticides (OPs). Herein, the metal-pyrimidine nanocubes (MPNCs) with intrinsic pH-switchable POD-like and catalase (CAT)-like properties were synthesized via the coordination of pyrimidin-2-ol with Cu2+. Meanwhile, acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and choline oxidase (CHO) were simultaneously encapsulated in MPNCs to construct an enzyme-nanozyme cascade catalytic platform (AChE/CHO@MPNCs). AChE/CHO@MPNCs could catalyze the hydrolysis of acetylcholine to choline, which was subsequently converted to H2O2. The POD-like activity of MPNCs was dominant under acidic conditions, while the CAT-like activity prevailed under neutral and alkaline conditions, which could catalyze H2O2 to •OH and O2, respectively, then oxidizing dopamine (DA) to polydopamine quantum dots (PDA QDs) with different fluorescence characteristics. Consequently, OPs could be detected in a linear range from 0.05 to 1000 nM with a LOD of 0.015 nM in acidic environments and a linear range from 0.05 to 500 nM with a LOD of 0.023 nM in alkaline environments. Overall, our work expands the horizon of constructing enzyme@MOFs composites with high catalytic activity. Meanwhile, the intrinsic pH-switchable multienzyme-like property opens avenues to construct sensing platforms with broad-pH-responsive for OPs and other analytes detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the large-scale production and utilization of organophosphorus pesticides (OPs), the great threat of OPs residues to the environment and public health has raised widespread concern in developing sensitive and rapid detection technology[32,37]. Among various OPs detection methods, fluorescent biosensing based on natural enzymes as target recognizers and signal generators has received more attention due to the merits of miniaturization, simplicity, and economy[42,47,9] and the high specificity, sensitivity, and efficiency of the natural enzyme[3,34]. The widely used strategy is constructing enzyme-nanozyme cascade catalytic platforms by acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and choline oxidase (CHO) combined with peroxidase (POD)-like[10,46,48]. However, some defects are still inevitable. For instance, almost all POD-like nanomaterials only exhibit single enzyme catalytic ability and can only function under acidic conditions Therefore, the application of enzyme-nanozyme cascade catalytic sensing platforms in broad-pH-response is still a challenge.

In recent years, some efforts have been made to enhance the catalytic activity of peroxidase mimics at neutral pH[30,41,46]. However, it is still not possible to function under acidic, neutral, and alkaline conditions. To break the limitation of pH conditions, pH-switchable multienzyme-like nanomaterials have been synthesized. Such nanomaterials exhibited POD-like properties under acidic conditions, which can catalyze the hydrolysis of H2O2 to •OH. As the pH increases, the catalase (CAT)-like property prevails and the POD-like property gradually vanishes, making H2O2 tend to be decomposed as O2 [43]. Such property makes pH-switchable multienzyme-like nanomaterials applicable in biosensing. For instance, Liang et al. achieved sensing detection of illegal additives under pH 3–11 using polyethyleneimine-coated Prussian blue nanocubes[25]. Hong et al. synthesized N-and B-doped fullerene, which is promising in catalyzing oxygenate intermediates for H2O2 decomposition under acidic, neutral, and alkaline conditions[13]. However, the activity of natural enzymes that function under neutral conditions will be inhibited under acidic or alkaline conditions when constructing enzyme-nanozyme cascade catalytic sensing platforms[7,18]. Furthermore, the pH mismatch makes it hard to realize one-pot detection under a single pH condition[2].

To circumvent this shortcoming, significant research has been conducted in constructing a biomimicking microenvironment to protect the enzymes, thus their catalytic activities can be highly maintained under unfavorable conditions[4,15]. As an important example of this strategy, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), synthesized by linking metal ions nodes and organic ligands, have the advantages of high specific surface area, tailorable pore channels, and balance the flexibility and rigidity that resemble the cellular environment[8,16]. However, enzyme activity retention is relatively low due to competing coordination and unfolding effects caused by organic ligands during the encapsulation[5,6,27]. A large amount of work has been done to improve enzymatic activity retention, such as chemical modification of the amino acids of the enzymes to control embedding patterns[5,6], fine-tuning the micro-environment[22,24], peptide-directed strategies[5,6], using customized enzyme activators[1]. Although these strategies hold great promise in the construction of nanosensors, it is still of great significance to explore the physically induced enhancement of enzyme activity that does not require tedious steps. DNA base derivatives have great potential as organic ligands to synthesize MOFs for enzyme immobilization due to their good biocompatibility[11,38]. Thus, it is speculated that using DNA base derivatives as organic ligands to synthesize MOFs with pH-switchable POD-like and CAT-like properties and use these MOFs as carriers for in situ enzyme immobilization. It can not only enhance the stability of the enzyme but also improve activity retention. At the same time, combining optical probes that can respond to products generated through cascade catalytic reactions under both acidic, neutral, and alkaline conditions can achieve a broad-pH-responsiveness to the targets.

Based on this assumption, the MOFs named metal-pyrimidine nanocubes (MPNCs) were synthesized through the coordination of DNA base derivative (pyrimidin-2-ol) with Cu2+. MPNCs exhibited intrinsic pH-switchable POD-like and CAT-like catalytic activities. The POD-like activity was dominant under acidic conditions in contrast the CAT-like activity prevailed under neutral and alkaline conditions. Meanwhile, MPNCs were used as carriers for in situ enzyme immobilization. The biocompatibility of pyrimidin-2-ol, the mild synthetic conditions, and the hydrophilicity of the MPNCs were favorable to enzyme activity retention. On this basis, acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and choline oxidase (CHO) were simultaneously encapsulated in MPNCs to construct an enzyme-nanozyme cascade catalytic sensing platform (AChE/CHO@MPNCs) and integrated with in-situ self-assembly of polydopamine quantum dots (PDA QDs) for broad-pH-responsive sensing assay for OPs ( Scheme 1). Dopamine (DA) can be quickly oxidated and polymerized to form fluorescent PDA QDs by the acceleration of •OH and O2 produced from the reaction of acetylcholine (ACh) with AChE/CHO@MPNCs at different pH values. Furthermore, PDA QDs have favorable dispersibility, excellent biocompatibility, and superior fluorescence properties[22,24,29]. Based on this, direct detection of OPs can be achieved with good specificity and sensitivity regardless of whether the pH of the actual sample is acidic, neutral, or alkaline.
The reagents, apparatus, and detailed experimental procedures are provided in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of MPNCs and AChE/CHO@MPNCs
ce:section.ce:para: An ideal in situ enzyme immobilization platform requires mild synthetic conditions (e.g., aqueous environment and room temperature)[45] and biocompatible precursors. Based on this, the MPNCs were synthesized via the reaction of CuSO4 and pyrimidin-2-ol in an aqueous phase at room temperature. The obtained MPNCs were purple powder with dispersed cubic-shaped nanostructures(Fig. S1A and S1B). The weight of the MPNCs was 4.01 mg and the yield was 25.18%. It was worth noting that the particle size was closely correlated to the proportion and concentration of the precursors. The higher the concentration of Cu2+ and pyrimidin-2-ol or the ratio of pyrimidin-2-ol to Cu2+ the smaller size of the particles could be obtained (Fig. S1B). The growth and nucleation rates determine the particle size. Smaller particles are produced by greater nucleation rates because growth slows down quickly and eventually stops for want of nutrients. The extra organic ligand is necessary to hasten the nucleation process[35]. To demonstrate the proof-of-concept, MPNCs were chosen as the immobilization carrier and AChE and CHO as representative enzymes. By a simple one-pot method, the enzymes were added to the aqueous solution containing CuSO4 and pyrimidin-2-ol. After stirring for 1 h at room temperature, the biocomposites (AChE/CHO@MPNCs) could be obtained with good morphology and crystallinity. The SEM and TEM images revealed that the as-synthesized AChE/CHO@MPNCs also display the same morphology as pristine MPNCs ( Fig. 1A and B). The composition, structure, and chemical states were analyzed by EDX, XRD, FTIR, and XPS. The elemental composition (Cu, C, N, and O elements) and distribution in MPNCs were revealed by the different colors of individual MPNCs in energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) (Fig. 1C). In XRD spectra (Fig. 1D), the diffraction peaks of the AChE/CHO@MPNCs overlapped the standard Bragg reflections of MPNCs, demonstrating no noticeable change in the MPNCs crystal structure after the immobilization of enzymes. FTIR spectra of pure enzymes, MPNCs, and AChE/CHO@MPNCs were depicted in Fig. 1E. The AChE/CHO@MPNCs exhibited FTIR peaks of MPNCs and enzymes simultaneously. This result demonstrated that the enzymes had been successfully loaded in MPNCs. The composition and chemical states of the AChE/CHO@MPNCs were further analyzed with the aid of XPS. The survey spectra exhibited four peaks at 936.5, 532.7, 400.0, and 284.7 eV corresponding to Cu2p, O1s, N1s, and C1s spectra, respectively (Fig. 1F). The C1 s XPS spectrum was fitted into four peaks assigning to CO (293.6 eV), C-N (291.1 eV), C-O/CN (287.5 eV), and C-C/CC (284.6 eV) (Fig. 1G). High-resolution XPS spectra of the N1s peak revealed two peaks of pyrrolic-N (404.6 eV) and pyridinic-N (400.9 eV) species in the nanocomposite, which indicated that the enzyme has been introduced into the MPNCs (Fig. 1H). The peaks at 537.1 eV and 532.5 eV could be assigned to the OC and O-C (Fig. 1I). Two peaks in the Cu2p spectra were observed and identified as Cu2p1/2 and Cu2p3/2. (Fig. 1J). The Cu2p spectra also demonstrated the presence of two valence states of Cu. The shakeup satellite peaks at 959.6.6 eV and 939.8 eV were assigned to Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 electrons of Cu2+, while the binding energies at 954.6 eV (2p3/2) and 934.74 eV (2p1/2) assigned to Cu+ (Fig. 1J). The simultaneous presence of Cu2+ and Cu+ plays an important role in the enzyme-like properties of MPNCs.

To demonstrate that both AChE and CHO were immobilized in MPNCs, FITC-labeled AChE (Green) and RhB-labeled CHO (Red) were used as building blocks for the construction of AChE/CHO@MPNCs. The green- and red-emission dots in CLSM images represented the distribution of AChE and CHO in MPNCs, respectively (Fig. S3). This result confirmed the simultaneous immobilization of two enzymes in MPNCs. A PVP surface adsorbent exchange experiment was carried out to exclude the possibility of surface adsorption. There were no FL emission peaks of the enzymes were detected in the supernatant after PVP treatment (Fig. S4A). In addition, the catalytic activities of AChE/CHO@MPNCs were also not affected after the treatment with PVP (Fig. S4B). These results demonstrated that AChE and CHO were indeed encapsulated within MPNCs rather than adsorbed on the surface. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm and pore size distribution curves of MPNCs and AChE/CHO@MPNCs were investigated. As in Fig. 1K, the corresponding isotherm of MPNCs and AChE/CHO@MPNCs were identified as type Ⅳ isotherm by the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) classification, which demonstrated the as-prepared MPNCs possess mesoporous structure[39]. The BET-specific surface area of MPNCs was 628.5 m2/g and the total pore volume of MPNCs was 1.39 cm3/g calculated by the density functional theory (DFT) method. Larger specific surface area and higher total pore volume are conducive to higher enzyme loading capacity. As expected, after the encapsulation of enzymes, the specific surface area and the total pore volume of the AChE/CHO@MPNCs decreased to 453.7 m2/g and 0.87 cm3/g, respectively. The decreased specific surface area and total pore volume of AChE/CHO@MPNCs revealed that the additional enzymes occupied a portion of pore volume and the encapsulated enzymes increased composite density. Compared with pure MPNCs crystal, the average pore width of AChE/CHO@MPNCs increased from 3.1 nm to 3.4 nm (Fig. 1L), which may be attributed to the introduction of enzymes resulting in structural defects[14]. Moreover, the mesoporous structure facilitated proton diffusion and improved reaction efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of enzymic loading capacity, immobilization efficiency, and catalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para: The challenge for enzyme immobilization-mediated signal amplification strategy is loading large amounts of enzymes and highly maintaining their catalytic activities. Accordingly, after a series of optimization of synthesis conditions including enzyme ratio, enzyme concentration, and synthesis time (Fig. S5), the loading capacity and immobilization efficiency for AChE and CHO were calculated by measuring the relative amounts of enzymes in MPNCs (Fig. S6). As a result, the loading capacity for AChE and CHO was 4.3% and 20.6%, respectively. The immobilization efficiency of AChE and CHO was 60.7% and 72.8%, respectively. Biocompatible organic ligands and mild synthesis conditions play an important role in the retention of enzymatic activity. As shown in Fig. 2A, after the mix of the two enzymes with pyrimidin-2-ol solution, the catalytic activities of the two enzymes were almost identical compared with the corresponding free enzymes. It indicated that pyrimidin-2-ol did not inhibit the enzyme activity. The MPNCs were synthesized at ambient temperature and pressure in the aqueous phase. Moreover, the pH of the mixture fluctuated in a smaller range near neutral during the synthesis. These conditions were beneficial to the preservation of enzymatic activity. It is known that proteins have a greater affinity for hydrophobic surfaces[31] and those hydrophobic interactions typically engender conformational changes, leading to protein denaturation and loss of activity[49]. To determine the hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity of the MPNCs, the water drop contact angle was measured. MPNCs were hydrophilic materials because the contact angle of water droplets was 52.6º (Fig. 2B). More hydrophilic nanomaterials will be more compatible with enzymes and result in retaining high enzymatic activity[26,28]. To explore the tertiary structure changes of AChE and CHO, the AChE@MPNCs and CHO@MPNCs were analyzed by FL spectroscopy[20,23]. The tryptophan-containing enzyme can emit an FL signal at a maximum emission wavelength (λ max) of 334 nm[26,28]. The tryptophan-derived FL emission of enzymes is sensitive to enzyme conformation[26,28]. Changes in enzyme conformation, such as unfolding, can lead to a red-shift of the λ max. Therefore, changes in enzyme conformation can be judged by monitoring the shift in λ max of the enzyme[36]. Compared to the free enzyme, there were no noticeable shifts in λ max of AChE@MPNCs and CHO@MPNCs after the encapsulation (Fig. 2C). These data reveal that the tertiary structure of AChE and CHO had no disturbance after being encapsulated in MPNCs crystal. The circular dichroism (CD) bands assigned to the α-helix (222 nm) and β-sheet (195 nm) of the enzymes[12] were maintained after exposure to pyrimidin-2-ol solution and after the encapsulation in MPNCs (Fig. 2D and 2E), indicating enzymes maintained their native conformations. Then, the activities of encapsulated AChE and CHO in AChE/CHO@MPNCs were investigated (Fig. 2F). Compared with free enzymes, AChE/CHO@MPNCs retained 93.5% and 92.6% of their initial activities, respectively. The slightly decreased activities of immobilized AChE and CHO may be due to mass transfer restriction in the scaffold. The biocompatibility of pyrimidin-2-ol, the mild synthetic conditions, and the good hydrophilicity of MPNCs allowed the immobilized enzymes to maintain high catalytic activities. This spontaneous process can serve as a general strategy to encapsulate various enzymes in MOFs and retain higher catalytic activity, which is more convenient compared to existing methods. They are multistep processes or require chemical reactions, which may result in loss of activity. Physically induced enhancement of catalytic activity in our work is more favorable to enzyme immobilization because of its mild synthesis condition and no additional steps required. These results indicated that MPNCs were ideal immobilized enzyme carriers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-switchable POD-like and CAT-like properties of MPNCs
ce:section.ce:para:
Besides the high retention of natural enzyme catalytic activity, MPNCs also exhibited a unique characteristic of pH-switchable POD-like and CAT-like activity. When the pH changed from acidic to alkaline, MPNCs displayed POD-like and CAT-like activity, respectively. As displayed in Fig. 3A, the MPNCs could catalyze the oxidation of TMB to generate blue oxTMB in the presence of H2O2 in acidic conditions. While neither H2O2 nor the MPNCs mixed with TMB alone can undergo color reaction, revealing that MPNCs indeed exhibit intrinsic POD-like activity[40]. The POD-like characteristic of MPNCs was investigated under different pH (3−12) using H2O2/TMB as the substrate. The color of the reaction system deepened as the pH increased from 3 to 5. While the color depth tended to decrease as the pH exceeded 5. It was worth mentioning that the blue color disappeared under neutral and alkaline conditions but observable bubbles appeared in the solution (Fig. 3B). The generated gas was verified to be O2 by a dissolved oxygen electrode. The corresponding UV–vis spectra indicated that MPNCs displayed POD-like activity in acidic conditions with the highest activity at pH 5 (Fig. 3C). Furthermore, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), was chosen as the probe to detect the oxygen-related radicals in the MPNCs-H2O2 reaction by the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments. The spin trap DMPO could capture the short-lived and highly reactive •OH (hydroxyl radical) to form DMPO-•OH spin adducts with the characteristic spectra of 1:2:2:1 intensity. As shown in Fig. 3D, the DMPO-•OH spin adducts were observed in the MPNCs-H2O2 reaction, indicating that MPNCs were competent in catalyzing H2O2 to generate •OH. EPR spectrum further supported that the MPNCs can catalyze H2O2 to generate •OH, illustrating the mechanism of catalytic activity of MPNCs via Fenton-like reactions[25,33]. The possible reaction mechanism could be described as follows:
2 Cu
+
+
H
2
O
2
+
2 H
+
→
2 Cu
2 +
+ •OH +
H
2
O
;
2 Cu
2 +
+
H
2
O
2
+ →
2 Cu
+
+
O
2
+ 2
H
+
;
TMB + •OH +
H
+
→
oxTMB
+
+
H
2
O
. The POD-like activity was further evaluated at pH 5 through steady-state kinetic experiments[17]. In a certain range of substrate concentration, the maximum reaction velocity (V max) and Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) were obtained by fitting the data into the Michaelis-Menten equation for both H2O2 and TMB (Fig. S7). A higher V max value normally denotes more potent catalytic activity, while a lower K m value implies a higher affinity of the enzyme for the substrate. The calculated K m values of MPNCs using H2O2 and TMB as substrates were 1.07 and 0.16 mM, and the V max values were 51.82 × 10−8 M·s−1 and 23.35 × 10−8 M·s−1, respectively. The K m values were comparable with that of HRP, indicating that MPNCs exhibited a significantly stronger affinity toward both TMB and H2O2 (Table S1). While the V max was much higher than that of HRP. Besides, compared with other nanomaterials with multi-enzyme activity, MPNCs exhibited comparable catalytic activity, which was beneficial to sensitive sensing detection.

As for the CAT-like activity of MPNCs, the redox potential of H2O2/O2 declined significantly with the pH increased, making H2O2 tend to be decomposed into O2 [43]. Therefore, the CAT-like activity prevailed while the POD-like activity gradually vanished with the increase in pH value. The corresponding reaction equations are as below.
2 Cu
2 +
+
H
2
O
2
+
2 OH
−
→
2 Cu
+
+
O
2
+
2 H
2
O
;
2 Cu
+
+
H
2
O
2
→
2 Cu
2 +
+
2 OH
−
. The CAT-like activity of MPNCs was further investigated by monitoring the reaction of H2O2 with MPNCs under different pH (3−12). As shown in Fig. 3E, there were no bubbles generated at pH 3–6. The amounts of bubbles showed an obvious upward tendency when the pH increased from 7 to 12, and the bubbles can be rapidly generated after mixing H2O2 with MPNCs for 5 s. Then the oxygen solubility was detected with an oxygen electrode. As shown in Fig. 3F, there was no additional generation of dissolved oxygen at pH 3–6, but it significantly increased when pH increased from 7 to 12. The corresponding dissolved oxygen showed a maximum value at pH 11, indicating that MPNCs exhibited the highest CAT-like activity at pH 11. Remarkably, under higher pH levels, the oxygen solubility increased with the concentration of MPNCs and H2O2 (Fig. S8A and S8B). This result demonstrated that the CAT-like activity of MPNCs was linearly dependent on the concentration of MPNCs and H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of AChE/CHO@MPNCs with pH-switchable multienzyme-like characteristic for broad-pH-responsive sensing
ce:section.ce:para: With the pH-switchable CAT-like and POD-like activities of MPNCs, a broad-pH-responsive fluorescent biosensing platform was constructed for the fluorometric assay of OPs. AChE/CHO@MPNCs with the activity of AChE and CHO could catalyze ACh hydrolysis to choline, which was subsequently converted to H2O2.

In acidic environments, the POD-like activity of MPNCs was dominant, and the generated H2O2 acting as the substrate for MPNCs can be catalyzed to •OH. As depicted in Fig. 4A, PDA QDs with prominent FL signals could be in situ generated by oxidative DA in the presence of •OH. The DA cannot be auto-oxidized in such an acidic environment, and such that no FL signal was found. When DA was mixed with ACh or AChE/CHO@MPNCs, no FL signal was observed as well. When OPs were present, the activity AChE would be inhibited and further suppressed the generation of •OH, resulting in a decrease in the FL intensity of PDA QDs. This result demonstrated that the AChE/CHO@MPNCs with POD-like characteristics facilitated the in-situ formation of fluorescent PDA QDs, which could be used in the biosensing detection of OPs in acidic environments. The following was the potential formation mechanism of PDA QDs. Firstly, DA was rapidly oxidized and rearranged to form 5,6-dihydroxyindole (DHI) in the presence of •OH. Then, DHI-derived oligomers with various polymerization degrees were produced under the further self-polymerization reaction. Finally, these diverse intermediate oligomers, such as pentamers, tetramers, and trimers were assembled to form the fluorescent PDA QDs through hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking [22,24]. The TEM image displayed the diameters of PDA QDs in the range of 2–7 nm with an average size of 4.15 ± 1.16 nm (Fig. 4B). The high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 4C revealed the PDA QDs with 0.21 nm lattice spacing were in accordance with that of the in-plane lattice spacing of graphitic carbon (100 facet)[19]. The optical properties of the PDA QDs were further characterized by UV–vis absorption and FL spectroscopy. As exhibited in Fig. 4D, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of PDA QDs contained a distinctive peak at 480 nm. The FL emission wavelength of PDA QDs was excitation-independent as the excitation wavelength varied from 350 nm to 390 nm (Fig. S9). This excitation-independent feature was attributed to the relatively homogenous particle size. The maximum FL intensity at 490 nm was obtained as the excitation wavelength at 360 nm. As depicted in Fig. 4E, in 3D FL plots, the PDA QDs showed a single emission center at 490 nm under the excitation of 360 nm. The relative quantum yields (QYs) of PDA QDs were measured to be 13.3% using quinine sulfate as a standard reference. The higher QYs was attributed to the presence of N atoms in DA.

In alkaline or neutral environments, the CAT-like property of MPNCs prevailed while the POD-like activity vanished. The generated H2O2 acting as the substrate for MPNCs can be catalyzed to O2. DA was chosen as a probe to detect oxygen levels due to its unique oxygen-sensitive and its colored product[21]. DA could self-polymerize to PDA QDs with FL emission through covalent bonding and cyclization in the presence of sufficient O2 under alkaline or neutral conditions[20,23,29]. As depicted in Fig. 4A, PDA QDs with prominent FL signals could be generated by oxidative DA in the presence of O2 produced by the catalytic hydrolysis of ACh by MPNCs. When DA was mixed with ACh or AChE/CHO@MPNCs, no FL signal was observed. When OPs were present, the activity AChE would be inhibited and further suppressed the generation of O2, resulting in a decrease in the FL intensity of PDA QDs. This result demonstrated that the AChE/CHO@MPNCs with CAT-like characteristics facilitated the in-situ formation of PDA QDs, which could be used in the biosensing detection of OPs in alkaline and neutral environments. The morphologies of the PDA QDs were characterized by TEM. As revealed in Fig. 4F, the PDA QDs were well monodispersed and spherical with an average diameter of 3.99 ± 0.95 nm. The lattice spacing was 0.21 nm (Fig. 4G). The optical properties of the PDA QDs were also determined by UV–vis absorption and FL spectra. PDA QDs also exhibited excitation-independent emission properties (Fig. S9), and there was a UV–vis absorption peak at the wavelength of 405 nm (Fig. 4H). Meanwhile, there was a single emission center located at a wavelength of 600 nm in the 3D FL plots when excited at a wavelength of 500 nm (Fig. 4I). The QYs of PDA QDs were determined to be 10.5% using quinine sulfate as a reference.

Further investigation was conducted on the fluorescence characteristics of PDA QDs in the detection systems with pH ranging from 3 to 12. As in Fig. S10, PDA QDs showed fluorescent emission under all pH conditions. Furthermore, PDA QDs exhibited different fluorescence intensities at 490 nm when pH ranged from 3 to 6, and at 610 nm when pH ranged from 7 to 12. Furthermore, the activity of free AChE and CHO decreased sharply under acidic and alkaline conditions, while the enzymes in AChE@MPNCs and CHO@MPNCs exhibited excellent stability (Fig. S11). Thus, the broad-pH-responsive sensing can be achieved by integrating an enzyme-nanozyme (with pH-switchable multienzyme-like activity) cascade catalytic platform with in-situ self-polymerization of PDA QDs with different FL emissions characteristics at different pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance in fluorescent biosensing for OPs detection
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, parathion methyl (PM, the main ingredient of OPs) was used as a model to explore the performance of AChE/CHO@MPNCs in fluorescent biosensing by measuring the FL intensity of PDA QDs. Because MPNCs exert their maximum POD-like and CAT-like activities at pH 5 and 11, respectively, these pH values were used as environmental conditions for studying the later detection performance. To achieve excellent performance, the conditions including ACh concentration, DA concentration, reaction time, and incubation time of OPs were optimized (Fig. S12). It was discovered that the signal response can reach a maximum in both acidic conditions (pH=5) and alkaline conditions (pH=11) when the concentration of DA, reaction time, concentration of ACh, and incubation time of OPs were 6 mM, 15 min, 10 mM, and 10 min, respectively, which were later used in the optical sensing for OPs detection. Under optimal detection conditions, the FL intensity of PDA QDs showed a gradual decrease with the increase in PM concentrations. In the acidic environment, the FL intensity (F) of PDA QDs at 490 nm was linearly related to the logarithm of PM concentration in a range of 0.05–1000.0 nM ( Fig. 5A). The regression equation was F= −137.12 log C+ 551.79 (R2 =0.964) (Fig. 5B), in which the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.015 nM (S/N = 3). In the alkaline environment, the FL intensity of PDA QDs at 610 nm linearly decreased in a range of 0.05–500.0 nM (Fig. 5C), in which the regression equation was F = - 87.89 log C+ 306.70 (R2 =0.989), and the LOD was 0.021 nM (S/N = 3) (Fig. 5D). Thus, AChE/CHO@MPNCs with the pH-switchable multienzyme-like property could be successfully used in the broad-pH-response fluorescent biosensing for OPs sensitive detection. It breaks the pH-limitation of traditional single POD-like that can only be detected under acidic conditions. Besides, in comparison to earlier work, this biosensing strategy exhibited a wider linear range and a lower LOD (Table S2), which was more favorable for sensitive sensing detection. It's worth noting that our work expanded the types of immobilized enzyme carriers with high catalytic activity retention. In addition, our assay only needed 25 min to achieve rapid detection of OPs and showed higher sensitivity than the reported detection methods. Meanwhile, our work can achieve detection in acidic, neutral, and alkaline actual samples (Table S2).

Specificity and anti-interference performance are important evaluation criteria for the biosensor, thus several non-OPs (thiamethoxam, dichlorophenol, methiocarb, acetamiprid, fenclorim, and diethofencarb) prepared in the concentration of 50 nM were used to investigate the specificity. As shown in Fig. 5E, only PM could cause a significant change in FL intensity both in the acidic environment and alkaline environment, indicating that AChE/CHO@MPNCs have excellent specificity for OPs. Besides, we also evaluated the anti-interference performance of AChE/CHO@MPNCs after the addition of biological reagents and common chemical electrolytes (500 nM) such as tartaric acid (TA), fructose, citric acid (CA), GSH, BSA, ascorbic acid (Vc), FeCl3, (NH4)2SO4, Cu3(PO4)2, MgSO4, CaCl2, NaCl, and K2CO3 (Fig. 5F). The results showed that no matter whether these interfering substances coexist with PM (50 nM) or not, there was no significant change in the fluorescence intensity of the detection system, indicating that AChE/CHO@MPNCs exhibited favorable anti-interference performance for screening OPs. This platform was universal for detecting a variety of OPs such as chlorpyrifos, dichlorvos, dimethoate, and diazinon (Fig. 5G). In virtue of excellent catalytic properties, the capacity of AChE/CHO@MPNCs to preserve the enzymes from harsh environments was further investigated (Fig. 5H). As anticipated, AChE/CHO@MPNCs exhibited good resistance to elevated temperatures, urea (chaotropic agent), protease, EDTA (chelating agent), and organic solvents (DMSO and DMF). Meanwhile, 89.3% catalytic activity for AChE/CHO@MPNCs was maintained after storing at room temperature for 15 days, while the free enzyme solution decreased by 38.6%. When the time was extended to 30 days, AChE/CHO@MPNCs remained at 76.4%, while the free enzymes almost completely lost their activities. Under acid and alkaline detection conditions, the immobilized enzymes still maintained above 86.4% of their initial activities, while the activities of free enzymes decreased to 50%. Such enhanced enzyme stability of the AChE/CHO@MPNCs was mainly attributed to the fact that the enzymes were confined within the nanoscale space, thus preventing enzyme inactivation and denaturalization[44]. Encouraged by the excellent stability of AChE/CHO@MPNCs, we investigated the recyclability, a critical performance metric in actual applications. AChE/CHO@MPNCs remained above 70% of initial catalytic activity even after 10 cycles. However, it was almost impossible for free enzymes to be recycled (Fig. 5I). Thus, as an enzyme immobilization carrier, MPNCs enabled the enzyme to exhibit superior reusability and stability against harsh environments, showing great potential for practical applications and long-term operations.

Subsequently, to estimate the reliability and applicability of AChE/CHO@MPNCs in actual environmental samples analysis. Neutral lake water and rice extracts were measured under neutral conditions, while acidic and alkaline wastewater from OPs production were measured under acidic and alkaline conditions, respectively. Furthermore, a commercial kit conformed to the GB/T5009.199–2003 standard was used as a reference to determine these samples. As summarized in Table S3, OPs were detected only in wastewater. These results showed good agreement with those of commercial kits. In the undetected samples, the performance of the sensing platform was evaluated using the standard addition method. PM standards were added to the samples at doses of 1.0, 10.0, and 100.0 nM, respectively. The designed system also exhibited good recoveries within 96.00%− 106.00% with relative standard deviations (RSD) lower than 5.65%, showing good practicability for OPs detection. Compared to the commercial kit, the relative errors were within ± 7.69%, demonstrating excellent precision of the AChE/CHO@MPNCs for OPs detection in complex matrices. In addition, the AChE/CHO@MPNCs from different preparation batches show excellent repeatability (Fig. S13). All the above results revealed the potential applicability of AChE/CHO@MPNCs for broad-pH-responsive sensing assay for OPs in practical applications including neutral water resources and agricultural products, as well as acidic and alkaline wastewater from the industrial production of OPs.

Herein, the metal-pyrimidine nanocubes (MPNCs) with intrinsic pH-switchable POD-like and CAT-like catalytic activities were synthesized via the coordination of pyrimidin-2-ol with Cu2+. Meanwhile, AChE and CHO were simultaneously encapsulated in MPNCs to construct an enzyme-nanozyme cascade catalytic platform for broad-pH-responsive sensing assay for OPs. The POD-like activity of MPNCs was dominant under acidic conditions in contrast the CAT-like activity prevailed under neutral and alkaline conditions. Meanwhile, the products hydrolyzed by this cascade catalytic platform at different pH can promote the in-situ self-polymerization of DA to PDA QDs with different fluorescence characteristics. Due to the high retention of enzyme activity after encapsulation and the protective effect of MPNCs on enzymes, direct detection of OPs can be achieved rapidly and sensitively regardless of whether the pH of the actual sample is acidic, neutral, or alkaline. It breaks the pH-limitation of traditional single POD-like that can only function under acidic conditions. Our work not only provides new insights into synthesizing pH-switchable multienzyme-like activity nanomaterials and enzyme@MOFs biocomposite with high activity retention but also opens avenues to construct broad-pH-responsive sensing assay for OPs and other analytes.

Changbao Gong: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Bo Chen: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yifei Xing: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Huimin Zhao: Validation, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that we have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21976022) and the Dalian POCT laboratory.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) residue is becoming more and more serious due to the increasing utilization in agricultural production. However, most of them are hard to degrade and remain in agricultural products or transfer to other food chains and ecosystems, causing severe threats to the environment and human health. Given their high toxicity and severe harm to public health security, many organizations and governments have paid great attention and they have enacted strict limits on drinking water, various agricultural, and related products. Therefore, developing reliable and facile methods for OPs residue detection turns out to be of great significance.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2023.132849.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
